title: Quick check: locks and auth codes

questions.prompt: A gaining registrar asks the client for the 'EPP code.' The losing registrar's panel shows a field labelled 'Authorization Key.' What's the relationship?
choices: They're different things; you need both.
They're the same thing. Auth code, EPP code, transfer code, and authorization key are interchangeable terms for the per-domain transfer secret.
EPP code is a newer standard; authorization key is being phased out.
The authorization key is encrypted; the EPP code is the decrypted form.
questions.explanation: The terminology drifts across registrars but the underlying value is one secret. Treat any of these terms as a synonym.

questions.prompt: You requested an auth code two weeks ago. The client just told you they want to initiate the transfer now. What do you do?
choices: Hand the two-week-old auth code to the gaining registrar; auth codes don't expire.
Request a fresh auth code from the losing registrar's panel before initiating.
Tell the client the transfer can't proceed because too much time has passed.
Try the old auth code first; if it fails, request a new one.
questions.explanation: Most registrars regenerate the auth code on each request and consume it on transfer; a code from two weeks ago may have been replaced or invalidated. Treat each auth code as single-use; if the original wasn't used quickly, regenerate.

questions.prompt: WHOIS for a domain shows `serverTransferProhibited` (not `clientTransferProhibited`). The client wants to transfer out. What's different?
choices: Nothing different; toggle it off in the registrar's panel.
The server-level lock is set by the registry and requires the registry's involvement to remove. This is escalation territory.
The lock will lift automatically after 60 days.
Bypass by transferring directly through ICANN.
questions.explanation: Server-level locks (serverTransferProhibited and friends) are set at the registry, not the registrar. Rare, usually applied to premium domains, government-related domains, or by special request. Removal requires escalation; you don't toggle them.
